It might be rephrased, but I agree that we need something with justification to express that this was a one-time-off (other frameworks make a lot of breaking changes with minor releases). Having a stable API gives us a lot trust and is often a major point for users. We had customer using 0.11 although 1.1 was released just because they were afraid of upgrading. Same for 1.0 users although 1.2 is released - we should make it our highest priority that we gain trust in our versioning and explain it in quite detail if we diverge from it. Ideally, we'd be like Apple and basically have everybody upgrade as soon as an update gets released. Otherwise, we'll always have to keep supporting old versions although we officially say that we don't.

I checked the logic, the reason for this change is the output number (if exclude min/max) of a OP maybe different for FP32 version and INT8 version for CPU, like pooling, for FP32 there may have 2 outputs (one for data one for workspace), but for INT8 version there is only 1 output (data). So here we need to get the quantized OP output number instead of original FP32 OP's. mirror_node is the quantized OP in this case and need to subtract 2 (for min/max) to get min/max's start index. I will add comment here to ease understanding.

See comment above: A user training using `zip(context, slices)` then doing`L.backward()` inside the loop won't have problems.

Also, suppose a user doesn't use this idiom and is unlucky (has `N` samples where `N % batch_size < num_slice`). At worst, MXNet warns them about the stale gradient; plus, it would occur on the very last batch. This is still preferable to doing training/inference just to error on the last batch with a `size < num_slice` message, requiring an ad-hoc workaround.

Depending on the use cases for this function `find_possible_roles`, this way of checking or adding superadmin role should be changed as it is not reliable.

I know this is how it was in legacy code, but I have noticed some serious problems with it. First of all the database row ID for the admin role should not always be assumed to be 1, or anything for that matter, as it can change on any instance. This check should therefore not base itself on the role id valie, but instead use the name of the role `"superadmin"` if this is even needed anymore. Because I guess if the `current_user` is superadmin, there is no point in checking anymore. E.g. `current_user.is_super_admin` or `current_user.is_admin` can tell this information directly.

So I suggest to drop the `always_add_superadmin` argument overall as I guess it is mostly pointless to check the user anymore if he is indeed an admin.

It looks like you intend that if `filter_fields` is None or [], then all fields should be updated. Two issues I have with that:

2. The logic on this line is a little tricky to follow.

One solution: in update_table, check that `properties` is a sequence. Here, make the default None, and near the top say
```
if filter_fields is None:
    filter_fields = all_fields
```

If this loop is a hot spot, we can make `rec.priority` an object with a `__get__` method, so that we can reference it once for the whole repodata set.  And then a single operation can switch the whole group.

Something like

```
class Priority(object):
    __slots__ = ('_priority',)
    def __init__(self, priority):
        self._priority = priority
    def __get__(self):
        return self._priority
```

Then in `process_repodata` line 422 could become

    repodata['_priority'] = priority = Priority(priority)

and instead of the for-loop here, we just have

    repodata['_priority']._priority = priority

Any reason you went with $uri rather than $request_uri? $uri is normalized, changing `/foo/../a` to `/a` (which doesn't matter so much, since browsers do that themselves) but also having some [vaguely documented differences around index files](http://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_core_module.html#var_uri) (which might matter, but I can't find a case where it does). My leaning is that $request_uri will have fewer surprises in store for us. (Their additional difference with regard to internal redirects shouldn't matter to us.)

I'm a bit confused here. Why do we strip the ending '/' only if the path is not `base_url + '/'`?

The documentation seems to imply that `If base_url is present, do not remove the ending '/'`, but the code does not remove the final `/` if there is a route beyond that base, ie `/base_url/foo/`.

How does this logic sound?

```python
def _clean_path(path, path_prefix):
  """Cleans the path of the request.

  Removes the ending '/' if the request begins with the path prefix and
  pings a non-empty route.
  
  Arguments:
    path: The path of a request.
    path_prefix: The prefix string that every route of this TensorBoard instance starts with.

  Returns:
    The route to use to serve the request (with the path prefix stripped if applicable).
  """
  regex = re.compile(path_prefix + ".+/$")
  if regex.match(path):
    # Remove the ending prefix.
    return path[-1]
  return path
```

thoughts on some alternate formats which I don't necessarily think are better but worth exploring other options.

- `scheme://package-name@authority/version`
- `scheme://package-name@authority?version=version` (less easy to type out by hand because of query parameter escaping)
- `scheme://authority/package-name?version=version` (less easy to type out by hand because of query parameter escaping)

In all three of these examples, the distinction between `package-name` and `version` is clearer as opposed to having to do string manipulation to separate them (as they are in distinctly different parts of the url scheme).

> I think in all of these, version should be optional, if left off, the *latest* version would be used.

This makes me think that we actually need to make actor methods *completely* ignore resources for now. The problem is, for example, if actor methods can acquire CPU resources, then when an actor method finishes, it has to trigger the dispatch of tasks, which we aren't doing. When we do make actor methods take resources into account, then we can't distinguish between dispatching actor methods and dispatching tasks, any time we do one, we need to do both.
